words: SAM SCHRAGER: What year were you born in?
CLARA PAYNE GROVE: 1879. May, 13. And I never have attributed any of the odds
and ends to the fact that it was the thirteenth!

speaker: SS
words: Had your parents homesteaded at all? Were they homesteading folks?

speaker: SS
words: How did they come to be in Idaho?

speaker: CG
words: I come of pioneer stock as far back as I know anything about; my family

speaker: CG
words: line. One pioneer was a nice, big, strapping young Irish lad of sixteen

speaker: CG
words: who had gone to the seaside to see the wonder of the boats; the ships.

speaker: CG
words: That undoubtedly was somewhere in 1700's. And a sailor approached him

speaker: CG
words: as he was on the wharf staring at the boats, and asked him if he'd like

speaker: CG
words: to see the inside of a boat. So they went on board. And the sailor

speaker: CG
words: showed him all about the deck, and he said, "Let's go below deck, and

speaker: CG
words: see what's down there." So they went below deck and they came to one

speaker: CG
words: door that was shut, and the boy said, "What's in here?" And the sailor

speaker: CG
words: said, "Go in and see." So he opened the door and let the boy in ahead

speaker: CG
words: of him and pulled the door shut and bolted it. And it wasn't unbolted

speaker: CG
words: until the ship was so far at sea there was no possibility of anybody

speaker: CG
words: swimming ashore. And he was put on deck with the hands and lived with

speaker: CG
words: the hands. And all the while, this was summertime, all the while they—

speaker: CG
words: the crew slept on deck. They just lay down on the deck and slept. Well,

speaker: CG
words: when they got to this side of the Atlantic, the boy knew about the plan

speaker: CG
words: tation owners indenturing a man. And indenturing means that they took

speaker: CG
words: him as a slave until he was twenty-one. At that time the plantation

speaker: CG
words: owner was supposed to give him a horse, bridle and saddle, and some

speaker: CG
words: times he did, and sometimes he didn't, as he chose. But being indent

speaker: CG
words: ured was enough of a slave that anyone attempting to run away was fair

speaker: CG
words: game for anyone who shot him down. That was not murder, that was mere

speaker: CG
words: ly capturing a slave.

speaker: CG
words: But, anyway, that's the Irish line which I al

speaker: CG
words: ways claim as my nationality. I have a mixture also of Swiss Dutch

speaker: CG
words: and of English and in late years I found out there was Scotch mixed in

speaker: CG
words: it. I hadn't known that until later years. But, on the other side,

speaker: CG
words: back in the 1700's an ancestor on my Swiss Du^ch side, in the forest

speaker: CG
words: around his house cut down such an enormous black walnut tree that he

speaker: CG
words: made a chest out of the boards that he made, and the boards were so

speaker: CG
words: large that the chest, which stood about two feet high, had no piecing

speaker: CG
words: in it. The cover, the bottom, the ends and the sides each was one board,

speaker: CG
words: No metal at all fastened it together. It was dovetailed together. And

speaker: CG
words: dovetailed so expertly and exactly that to this day that chest, I don't

speaker: CG
words: have it but my daughter does have it, to this day, that chest can be

speaker: CG
words: pulled apart and put back dovetailed together. There was that ancestry.

speaker: CG
words: And he made it as a wedding chest for his daughter, and immediately af

speaker: CG
words: ter the marriage ceremony the daughter and her nice husband with

speaker: CG
words: the chest in his two-wheeled, one ox cart, and they walking beside,

speaker: CG
words: started West from— now I'm not sure whether it was from Carolina or

speaker: CG
words: from Virginia—at any rate, it was back in 1700 before either state was

speaker: CG
words: divided. They started West and one place they came to the forest was

speaker: CG
words: so dense that they couldn't get through just as they were, so they took

speaker: CG
words: the chest off of the cart and took the ox loose from it and led the ox

speaker: CG
words: through for what trail that would make, then they turned the cart up on

speaker: CG
words: one wheel and carried the cart through, then they carried the chest

speaker: CG
words: through and put it back on the cart and went on West.

speaker: CG
words: They came to an

speaker: CG
words: other place that they couldn't get the chest through, so they pulled

speaker: CG
words: the dovetailing apart and laid the pieces and the contents out on the

speaker: CG
words: ground and led the ox through for what trail that would make, and then

speaker: CG
words: they went back and picked the chest up piece by piece and carried it

speaker: CG
words: piece by piece through. But still they went West. We have word of them

speaker: CG
words: in Indiana, where my mother and eleven other children of the same fam

speaker: CG
words: ily were born. And then from there they went to Minnesota and from

speaker: CG
words: there— well, they were there at the time of Indian war— but, they were

speaker: CG
words: never disturbed. At one time, perhaps not during the wartime, but at

speaker: CG
words: one time, an extremely cold winter morning, Grandfather got up and built

speaker: CG
words: the kitchen fire and put the teakettle on with what water was in it to

speaker: CG
words: get it boiling to thaw the pump so he could get a pail of water. So,

speaker: CG
words: when the teakettle boiled he took it out to the pump and just at that

speaker: CG
words: time an Indian came along, just one Indian alone, he was dressed solely

speaker: CG
words: in his breechclout, his mocassins and a blanket, and he asked if he

speaker: CG
words: might have a drink of water. And Grandfather told him, yes, as soon as

speaker: CG
words: he could get the pump thawed so he could get water. So, he went on and

speaker: CG
words: used what water he had to thaw the pump and got water. And he said to

speaker: CG
words: the Indian, "Aren't you cold?" And the Indian said, "Your face cold?"

speaker: CG
words: Grandfather said, "No, my face doesn't get "Indian all face!"

speaker: CG
words: And it may have been at that special time or some other time, Grandmother

speaker: CG
words: was having a rag carpet woven;,that was what you did for a carpet in

speaker: CG
words: those days, you saved everything you could to sew into carpetrags, and

speaker: CG
words: then you got the person who had the loom to weave your carpet. Well,

speaker: CG
words: Grandmother was having this carpet woven, and she said to Grandfather

speaker: CG
words: one day to saddle a horse for her, she wanted to ride over to the woman

speaker: CG
words: who had the loom and tell her exactly how to stripe the colors. And

speaker: CG
words: Grandfather said, ho,, the Indians were out, she couldn't go, that he

speaker: CG
words: would go. And Grandmother said, "No, you'd forget before you got there

speaker: CG
words: how I want it striped, and I want it like I want it, and I'm going. You

speaker: CG
words: saddle old Dolly for me, she's afraid of the Indians and I'll know if

speaker: CG
words: there are any about. So Grandfather saddled old Dolly and Grandmother

speaker: CG
words: mounted on the sidesaddle. And when she got home, she said she knew

speaker: CG
words: that in a certain clump of bushes beside the road there must have been

speaker: CG
words: an Indian, because old Dolly just refused to pass it, and she had quite

speaker: CG
words: a time to make Dolly pass that clump of bushes. So she knew there was

speaker: CG
words: an Indian in it. But that was nothing compared to striping a carpet!

speaker: CG
words: That sidesaddle was in our family until, oh, it's been more than forty

speaker: CG
words: years ago, my sister, who had the sidesaddle, died. The children by a

speaker: CG
words: of her husband, by former marriage, swarmed in and took what they

speaker: CG
words: wanted and burned what they didn't want. Whether they burned the side

speaker: CG
words: saddle or took it, we never knew. That was the last of the sidesaddle.

speaker: CG
words: Well, I came to Idaho; I came to Troy. The reason was, that the

speaker: CG
words: home of myself and my daughter, which was my family, lived in Moore,

speaker: CG
words: Montana, and we had come West; pioneer stock and didn't know anything,

speaker: CG
words: any of us except to go West. We came West to the home of a brother at

speaker: CG
words: Ruby, Washington, which is north of Spokane, north of Newport, Washing

speaker: CG
words: ton. He was away. We lived in his home for a time. And back in Moore,

speaker: CG
words: Montana, there had been a weekly newspaper that was edited by a man by

speaker: CG
words: named -Wilbur Johns. And my daughter had set type a little for

speaker: CG
words: him in the office and I had been his local correspondent for news, and

speaker: CG
words: I wrote to Mr. Johns at Moore, where I still owned $„. house, to know

speaker: CG
words: if he would have work on the paper for me if I returned to my home in

speaker: CG
words: fcftoore. In the meantime, unknown to me, Mr. Johns had come to Troy,

speaker: CG
words: Idaho and taken over the weekly newspaper there. And he answered me

speaker: CG
words: from Troy, saying that if I wanted to come to Troy, he would give me

speaker: CG
words: half day work in the office. So, daughter and I, after we had been

speaker: CG
words: as far West as the land goes, which is Sliverdale, across the narrow

speaker: CG
words: neck of land from Seattle, out in the Pacific, and we had been at Sil

speaker: CG
words: verdale keeping house there for some people for a time. And

speaker: CG
words: then we went to Troy for me to take this half day position in the week

speaker: CG
words: ly with Mr. Johns.

speaker: SS
words: Did he ever tell you why he came to Troy?

speaker: CG
words: No, he never did. I never asked him and he never told me why he went

speaker: CG
words: to Troy. But, I think it probably was because the little town of Moore

speaker: CG
words: had mostly dried up and blown away and times were hard there and I imagine

speaker: CG
words: he was not making a living off of his paper. So he just discon

speaker: CG
words: tinued it and went to Troy where the pickings were a little better. I

speaker: CG
words: am sure that was why.

speaker: CG
words: So, I worked half day  And in Troy, the women schoolteachers,

speaker: CG
words: secretaries and a store clerk got together and rented a furnished house

speaker: CG
words: and lived there, wanting a housekeeper. So, my daughter and I went

speaker: CG
words: there, she was in high school, and the next year graduated from the

speaker: CG
words: Troy High School, in 1927. We were in the house with the girls for a

speaker: CG
words: little while. Well, that just didn't do very well, because the child

speaker: CG
words: needed a home, which you don't get in one little upstairs room that

speaker: CG
words: isn't heated, nor in the kitchen. So, I got the loan of a cookhouse

speaker: CG
words: for a threshing crew that the man very kindly hauled up into the yard

speaker: CG
words: of the house where I was housekeeping. And my daughter and I lived in

speaker: CG
words: there and kept the house and did the cooking and so on for the girls.

speaker: CG
words: And we did that for the remainder of the year.

speaker: SS
words: Was your daughter able to help with the housework very much while she

speaker: SS
words: was going to school?

speaker: CG
words: Oh, she did help some, but it wasn't difficult because the cooking was

speaker: CG
words: practically the only part of it. And that was nothing to cook for half

speaker: CG
words: a dozen people. She did help, yes, but there just wasn't so much to do.

speaker: CG
words: It didn't bother me any at all to put in my half day at the office.

speaker: CG
words: She was busy with her high school, and I was busy with the office,

speaker: CG
words: and in our second year: One day Mr. Johns and I had been sticking type

speaker: CG
words: and we both met at the stone to empty our sticks at the same moment and

speaker: CG
words: as we emptied them Mr. Johns looked up at me and he said, "Do you want

speaker: CG
words: to buy this paper?" Well, that was the first I'd ever heard of such a

speaker: CG
words: thing, but just as though we had been talking about the weather.

speaker: CG
words: I said, "Yes, I do." He said, "Well, let's go to the bank and make out

speaker: CG
words: the papers." I said, "Alright, let's go." So we did. And as they were

speaker: CG
words: making out the papers, the banker— oh, you know his name—Ole Bowman,

speaker: CG
words: said, "And you will pay this amount every month." I said, "Well,

speaker: CG
words: make the contract that I will pay that amount and may pay as much more

speaker: CG
words: as I please." And, he said, "Oh, you won't need that." I said,"Well

speaker: CG
words: put it in the contract anyway," I said, "put it in." Well, he and Mr.

speaker: CG
words: Johns exchanged a little look between them of real amusement. But any

speaker: CG
words: way he put it in. There wasn't one payment but that I made the payment

speaker: CG
words: and a little more. Because the people seeing it under new hands swarm-

speaker: CG
words: ed in to hand their business to the Troy News. But any rag has it s

speaker: CG
words: own pet name. So, we were working on that. One day a man came in and

speaker: CG
words: offered me $500 for the plant and the building; everything, lock,stock

speaker: CG
words: and type. I told him "No, I wasn't interested." But I did begin to

speaker: CG
words: try to sell. And right out of the blue there came a man who at a form

speaker: CG
words: er time had had this same paper and given it up and gone elsewhere,

speaker: CG
words: and wanted to come back. And he came in and he offered me much more

speaker: CG
words: than the place was worth. It was said, for one thing, that the press

speaker: CG
words: was the one that Mark Twain had brought across the plains with him.

speaker: CG
words: And I always said, "That's pure nonsense, Mark Twain never brought any

speaker: CG
words: thing as useless as that old press across the plains!!" So I don't

speaker: CG
words: know whether or not it is the press that he brought. It was a huge thing

speaker: CG
words: it would have practically have filled that alcove. A huge thing.r I

speaker: CG
words: stood on a box, this high, to feed it by hand. But, anyway, we filled

speaker: CG
words: out the paper, and Mr. Johns as he was leaving said, "Now here's some

speaker: CG
words: boilerplate, you're going to need it when you have a vacant spot and

speaker: CG
words: nothing to put in it." I said, "Alright." Well, instead of having

speaker: CG
words: ever a vacant spot, I'd had it only just a little while, until I had

speaker: CG
words: to put out a supplement to have room to put all the news in! And I

speaker: CG
words: did really love that.

speaker: SS
words: Tell me how it changed under your hands; that it got to be so much big

speaker: SS
words: ger and that it got more business. What is it that you did that made

speaker: SS
words: you have to put out a supplement?

speaker: CG
words: Well, people bringing in news. Nobody could go to Moscow for a day of

speaker: CG
words: trading. Nobody could go to Spokane to spend an afternoon. Nobody

speaker: CG
words: could do anything, everybody brought me the news. And when you

speaker: CG
words: have a town newspaper, what the people want and demand and frequently

speaker: CG
words: can't get, is whether Mr. and Mrs. John Smith spent the day in Spokane

speaker: CG
words: or whether Lillian Smith took a prize at school, or every little thing

speaker: CG
words: that happens, is what they want to know. And those were the things that

speaker: CG
words: people were just pouring into the office, and I was printing all of it,

speaker: CG
words: because it's what they wanted. And if they wanted it, I wanted 'em to

speaker: CG
words: have it. So that is how it happened that I had to put out a supplement.

speaker: CG
words: And as for knowing anything about owning a paper, or the legal aspects

speaker: CG
words: that are necessary to be conformed with or how to feed the press, or

speaker: CG
words: much of anything else, I didn't know anything about it. Not anything.

speaker: CG
words: I knew how to be a good correspondent for news, yes, but it takes a lit

speaker: CG
words: tle more than that to put a paper into the post office!!

speaker: SS
words: Did you have to learn the rest, or did you have help for the rest?

speaker: CG
words: I had a woman there that Mr. Johns had wanted me to put on for help.

speaker: CG
words: She didn't know as much about it as I did. And she was just a real

speaker: CG
words: nuisance, and I found that she had come because it was supposed that

speaker: CG
words: after just a little her husband would get the paper for practically

speaker: CG
words: nothing, and she'd already be there in the office!! So, I just kept

speaker: CG
words: her a little while and she was highly indignant when I told her I

speaker: CG
words: couldn't keep her any longer. But she was making the work so much

speaker: CG
words: harder for me that— it was hard enough before. And then I couldn't

speaker: CG
words: lift the chase —well dumbly, it didn't occur to me to have the chase

speaker: CG
words: put on the press and kept there. Mr. Johns hadn't done it that way.

speaker: CG
words: He put everything in it on the stone and then carried it to the press

speaker: CG
words: and then put it back on the stone. And it didn't occur to me that that

speaker: CG
words: chase could just sit on the press all the time and I could fill it there

speaker: CG
words: as well as anywhere. So—well, he was one of the Olsons in Troy, which

speaker: CG
words: one I can't tell you now—who would come in on press day and take the

speaker: CG
words: chase to the press and later take it off and bring it back to the stone

speaker: CG
words: for me. So, we got along.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have regular correspondents?

speaker: CG
words: I had one, or possibly two, I'm not sure which, regular outside corres

speaker: CG
words: pondents. And Ethel and I both kept our ears wide for news all of the

speaker: CG
words: time, and people just came and brought it to me, because they were so

speaker: CG
words: happy to know what the news was. And I did enjoy it very much, but Ethel

speaker: CG
words: was graduating the second year; ready for university. And, we had de

speaker: CG
words: cided that we would go on with the paper and get a car and Ethel would

speaker: CG
words: drive from Troy to the University in good weather, or live in the hall

speaker: CG
words: through the winter. But, there began to be opposition to a woman own

speaker: CG
words: ing any business, and I wasn't hard enough— I've grown harder since, I

speaker: CG
words: hope— I wasn't hard enough to take it and tell them, "Well, object if

speaker: CG
words: you want to, I don't care!" So the opposition grew so pointed that I

speaker: CG
words: sold, when this man came along and offered me so much more than the

speaker: CG
words: place was worth.

speaker: SS
words: On what basis could these people be opposed to a woman owning a news

speaker: SS
words: paper?

speaker: CG
words: They were all of foreign extract. It was common saying that the post

speaker: CG
words: master and his family and myself were the only Americans in there!! It

speaker: CG
words: was the foreign element where women do not, at that time— see this was

speaker: CG
words: forty-eight years ago— that's quite a little while— and a great many

speaker: CG
words: of these people themselves had come across, not descendants, but^them

speaker: CG
words: selves. And it was just simple this foreign idea—

speaker: SS
words: In Europe and the old countries, you were starting to say, the women

speaker: SS
words: didn't have that kind of authority. Is that the case?

speaker: CG
words: No, women didn't own business. A woman didn't own a business. She

speaker: CG
words: owned her husband's name. That's not only in the foreign speaking peo

speaker: CG
words: ple— there was here in Moscow a woman whom I well knew; her husband

speaker: CG
words: was English and she said to me one time, she'd been asking me if I

speaker: CG
words: thought she should do this-that-or-the-other, and I said, "Yes." And

speaker: CG
words: she said, "Well, you know how it is, the English do not expect their

speaker: CG
words: women to have the-liberty that women in the United States have." So it

speaker: CG
words: isn't only the foreign speaking people who objected to women owning bus

speaker: CG
words: iness. But, according to her, an Englishman has an idea that a woman

speaker: CG
words: owns her husband's name and that is sufficient. Maybe it is!

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that the women in Troy supported your being the editor more

speaker: SS
words: than the men did?

speaker: CG
words: I think that the common people did. The trouble did not come from the

speaker: CG
words: common people; it came higher up than that.

speaker: SS
words: The prosperous ones like the farmers or the businessmen?

speaker: CG
words: Well, I think some of the businessmen did not object. I know I had one

speaker: CG
words: item about a woman who was actually the head in Troy of a business that

speaker: CG
words: was part of a business in Moscow at that time. It was the outlying Sta

speaker: CG
words: tion, and this woman was the business. There was no question on it.

speaker: CG
words: And I had a nice little write-up for her, something about a woman doing

speaker: CG
words: this work, and so capably. And a relative of her's came in— he did

speaker: CG
words: read me the riot act!! For writing that article. He said, "She is not

speaker: CG
words: the head of that!(chuckles) Well, she was and there was no denying it,

speaker: CG
words: but, anyway, this man who was related to her, wasn't going to have any

speaker: CG
words: body say that any woman could run a business!!

speaker: SS
words: How deep-seated do you think this idea was, that the women were supposed

speaker: SS
words: to be in the home and be sort of subservient?

speaker: CG
words: Well, I don't know where that idea came from, but I think it was always

speaker: CG
words: there. To me, this idea of trying to decide which is greater, male or

speaker: CG
words: female, is just exactly as though the steering wheel on an automobile,

speaker: CG
words: and one of the wheels under the automobile got into a discussion and

speaker: CG
words: each of them claimed to be the greater! And the steering wheel said to

speaker: CG
words: the wheel with the tire on it, "I'd like to know how you'd get where

speaker: CG
words: you want to go if it wasn't for me up here guiding you?" And the wheel

speaker: CG
words: with the tire comes right back, "Well, how would you get there in spite

speaker: CG
words: of all the steering you do if I didn't run?" So, for one to say it's

speaker: CG
words: any greater than the other, is nonsense. Each one in place is it,

speaker: CG
words: and that's all there is to it!!

speaker: SS
words: Where you were, were you in such a place to see the struggle for the

speaker: SS
words: right to vote for women? Did that come about?

speaker: CG
words: Yes, yes, I remember distinctly about that time, that there really was

speaker: CG
words: quite a struggle about women getting to vote. But they persevered and

speaker: CG
words: all the womens' organizations took it up and finally carried it over.

speaker: CG
words: And, I remember one campaign speech that I heard when the vote was new

speaker: CG
words: er than it is now, a good deal, and this woman was talking about the

speaker: CG
words: candidate "having a rainbow around his shoulders!" And I thought,

speaker: CG
words: "Well, that's a funny campaign talk." So I thought the women were sort

speaker: CG
words: of new at going at the business of voting and talking, and so on. I

speaker: CG
words: remember quite a number of things. I remember when Williams Jennings

speaker: CG
words: Bryan was so much in the public eye. My father— what I was going to

speaker: CG
words: say sort of slips from me. But, anyway, he was a politician first,

speaker: CG
words: and he was father afterwards, if there was any time left, but usually

speaker: CG
words: there wasn't much time left after the politics! So, I guess I come

speaker: CG
words: of my political activities honestly.

speaker: SS
words: What were your father's politics?

speaker: CG
words: He was straight Republican! There wasn't anything else on the face

speaker: CG
words: of the earth that was any good but a Republican! A Democrat, was just

speaker: CG
words: dirt, that was all!! We had the two parties for a while and we had

speaker: CG
words: various other parties. We had the Populist Party. It happened that

speaker: CG
words: an uncle of mine was a Populist, and he wasn't in our county, but in

speaker: CG
words: his county, there was one Populist vote, and since he was a Populist,

speaker: CG
words: of course, they would know who had voted it! And someone was razzing

speaker: CG
words: him about it, says, "Well, how do you feel now?" He said, "Oh, I feel

speaker: CG
words: just like a good egg in a bushel basket of bad ones!" But the Populist

speaker: CG
words: Party did make quite a mark for a while.

speaker: SS
words: Your father, as a Republican, what did he think of the La Follette Re

speaker: SS
words: publicans? What did he think of those progressive Republicans. The

speaker: SS
words: ones that wanted to reform the party?

speaker: CG
words: Oh no, no, no— no reform was needed, they were perfect like they were!

speaker: SS
words: He wasn't for the free silver and what Bryan

speaker: CG
words: I never heard him discuss silver. I do remember that I was at a gath

speaker: CG
words: ering where an outside orator— and he was a real orator— was discus

speaker: CG
words: sing the silver question, and he explained it, that it was just a

speaker: CG
words: matter of sixteen to one, and very simple, and went on explaining it,

speaker: CG
words: and he says, "If I had a boy of sixteen who couldn't understand that,

speaker: CG
words: I'd whip him." Well, I wondered how that'd make him understand it.

speaker: CG
words: (Chuckles) But, this was a real orator and one of his remarks stuck

speaker: CG
words: with me, I was just a kid at the time, and he said','They go around talk

speaker: CG
words: ing about prosperity, parity, parity!" And he could put over little

speaker: CG
words: things like that just instantly and sweep on into other things with such

speaker: CG
words: ease that I just sat there and admired the way he could talk. And I

speaker: CG
words: heard— I wonder if I can remember her name at all— she was one of the

speaker: CG
words: greatest advocates of the vote for women, and she spoke from the rost

speaker: CG
words: rum in our courtroom, we were the county seat, she spoke there, and I

speaker: CG
words: went to hear her. And I admired— she was a large woman, tall, and

speaker: CG
words: wide and thick— and I admired the way, without a particle of self con

speaker: CG
words: sciousness, she went up onto the rostrum and took chair there. And

speaker: CG
words: she took for her text, telling us just where in the Bible it is found,

speaker: CG
words: which I do not remember now, "Let no man take thy crown." I've always

speaker: CG
words: remembered how she could bring out that word,  But she gave a great

speaker: CG
words: talk, why women should vote.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember the arguments she used in that talk? What her reasoning

speaker: SS
words: was?

speaker: CG
words: I do not remember, excepting her giving the topic and in the way she

speaker: CG
words: did. But, at the time, I fully agreed with her, that she was perfectly

speaker: CG
words: right about women having the vote.

speaker: SS
words: Where was this? That you saw her? What was the town, and the state?

speaker: CG
words: Redfield, South Dakota.

speaker: SS
words: Did many speakers come through and speak to the people there?

speaker: CG
words: We had many speakers. We were a county seat town and we were a very

speaker: CG
words: large town when I first knew it. I think there might have been two

speaker: CG
words: thousand people there, and it grew and grew. And we had, what we cal

speaker: CG
words: led Redfield College. It was a denominational college of the Congre

speaker: CG
words: gational Church. And the campus consisted of one three story red brick

speaker: CG
words: building with the customary path, of course. And the basement, at first

speaker: CG
words: was just merely a storage place, but very soon, it was made into a kit

speaker: CG
words: chen and dining room and a cook was employed. The men students— that

speaker: CG
words: was the basement, then the first floor was the classrooms; the second

speaker: CG
words: floor was the dormitory for the men students; the third floor was the

speaker: CG
words: dormitory for the women students. Well, a women's organization in the

speaker: CG
words: town took it up that that was not the proper way to lodge students. That

speaker: CG
words: there had to be a separate building for a dormitory for the women stu

speaker: CG
words: dents. So, about a block from the one building that we had at first,

speaker: CG
words: they put up a two story building with a bedroom and a sitting room for

speaker: CG
words: the housemother, and I think it was two extra bedrooms, and the second

speaker: CG
words: floor had bedrooms, what it would hold, and that was sufficient for the

speaker: CG
words: women who had no other lodging.

speaker: CG
words: There was one student there that I always remember very distinctly.

speaker: CG
words: She had been to the college building, or near it, and then she came to

speaker: CG
words: our home, which was a block from the college building, and Mother took

speaker: CG
words: her in, and she was inquiring about how to get connected with the col

speaker: CG
words: lege. And Mother said, "Well, Reverend Beeton is the President, and

speaker: CG
words: Clara will take you over there to Reverend tfeeton and he will tell you

speaker: CG
words: all these things that you want to know." So, Clara listened carefully

speaker: CG
words: to her conversation with Mother, so that her ears didn't miss anything,

speaker: CG
words: and then she took the girl across town, clear to the other side, where

speaker: CG
words: the President Reverend Jeeton and his family lived. And when we got

speaker: CG
words: there to it, Mary Hart said, "Where is the house? Which one is it?"

speaker: CG
words: And I pointed, and I said, "That's the one." And she said, "Well, then

speaker: CG
words: you can go home now." Well, I didn't want to go home now, I wanted to

speaker: CG
words: take her right to the door. I wanted to know everything I could know.

speaker: CG
words: So, I kept going and she said, "You can go home now." And when we got

speaker: CG
words: somewhere near it, she said, "You'd better go home now." Well, I took

speaker: CG
words: the hint finally and went home. And she went on to see Reverend Jlfeeton.

speaker: CG
words: Well, she made out someway. I found out later that she had given out

speaker: CG
words: at her home on a farm near Aberdeen, which was forty miles or so north

speaker: CG
words: of us, that she was going to college and her father told her she could

speaker: CG
words: n't, that he had no money for her to go with. She said she was going

speaker: CG
words: anyway. She was going to college and nothing was going to stop her.

speaker: CG
words: So, when she was ready to go to college he gave her a ten dollar bill,

speaker: CG
words: and he said, "Now, Mary, don't ask me for anything more, I cannot give

speaker: CG
words: you another penny and pay the taxes too." He said, "That is all I can

speaker: CG
words: give you for the whole year." So, with her ten dollar bill she got her

speaker: CG
words: self to Redfield. And I don't know just where she lived or how she got

speaker: CG
words: by that year, but she did. And she put in her four years there. Her

speaker: CG
words: brother joining her for the two last years, and when the brother was

speaker: CG
words: with her, they got a room over the drugstore. It was a drugstore and

speaker: CG
words: and, oh, sort of a general store, in a way, and up above there were two

speaker: CG
words: rooms that had a stove and a couple of chairs and she and her brother

speaker: CG
words: lived up there and they lived practically without heat through the whole

speaker: CG
words: university-college year. It wasn't a university, it was a college. And

speaker: CG
words: for their supper they would build just fire enough that she could cook

speaker: CG
words: some supper and when that fire was gone there was no more fire until

speaker: CG
words: the next supper. She and her brother, on the two kitchen chairs would

speaker: CG
words: sit at the table with their books and one blanket around the two of

speaker: CG
words: them, so they could keep a little warmer, and there they did their work

speaker: CG
words: for the next day. And she took all that, and living entirely without

speaker: CG
words: money—and it was just terrible how destitute she was, but she graduat

speaker: CG
words: ed anyway. And I don't know what became of her and her brother, but I

speaker: CG
words: do know that she graduated, and we were all proud of her.

speaker: SS
words: Did you see her during those years?

speaker: CG
words: I saw her frequently. Yes, I saw her frequently. And I guess she nev

speaker: CG
words: er had more than money enough to barely subsist. But she did live in

speaker: CG
words: the girls' dormitory. And the girls' dormitory, of course, had a nick

speaker: CG
words: name, why not? It was the Cheese Factory! (Chuckles) That is the way

speaker: CG
words: it was known.

speaker: SS
words: Did she visit with the family? Is that how you saw her? Or did you

speaker: SS
words: used to go over to the college to see her. Where would you run into

speaker: SS
words: her during the school years?

speaker: CG
words: Where was I?

speaker: SS
words: No, where did you run into her? Did she come to the house and visit?

speaker: CG
words: She had come to our house, and we lived only a block from the college

speaker: CG
words: and at one time we lived a block in front of it, and then we had our

speaker: CG
words: own home a block sort of to one side of it. But going to and from town

speaker: CG
words: the students passed by our door as we lived in front of the building,

speaker: CG
words: and I'd see her, as she'd go back and forth. And sometimes I'd meet

speaker: CG
words: her here and there. Never had any close connection with her.

speaker: SS
words: You said a while ago, that the women's organization wanted the building

speaker: SS
words: of the new dormitory for the girls, and I'm wondering—were the women's

speaker: SS
words: organizations very active when you were young?

speaker: CG
words: Yes, they were. There weren't so many of them by any means. There was

speaker: CG
words: the Ladies Aid of the churches; that was one group. And that was it,

speaker: CG
words: for the women; was the Ladies Aid. But it was very active. I do not

speaker: CG
words: recall any name of any other womens organization.

speaker: SS
words: What about the Womens Christian Temperance Union?

speaker: CG
words: The Womens Christian Temperance Union was organized by Frances Willard

speaker: CG
words: in 1876. Next year is our centennial year. My Grandmother Connor was

speaker: CG
words: one of the first members of that group. My mother was a member of that

speaker: CG
words: group. I am a member of that group, and have been District President

speaker: CG
words: for several years, and for two years was state organizer with national

speaker: CG
words: recognition,to work across the border in Washington. That happened,

speaker: CG
words: because as I was driving from one place to another in Idaho, naturally,

speaker: CG
words: because the highway ran on both sides of the state line, I was sometimes

speaker: CG
words: in Washington and a group there would ask me for help there, and so I

speaker: CG
words: wrote to National and explained it to them and they gave me recognition

speaker: CG
words: to work in Washington as well as in Idaho.

speaker: SS
words: Your grandmother was one of the earliest people to—?

speaker: CG
words: She was one of the earliest of the members of the Womens Christian Tem

speaker: CG
words: perance Union.

speaker: SS
words: Did she ever speak to you about it? Did you know why she became a—

speaker: SS
words: she grew to want to join it?

speaker: CG
words: It's very plain to me (Chuckles) that you don't know the old-time pion

speaker: CG
words: eer women! They didn't speak,(Chuckles) they were quite a silent group!

speaker: CG
words: Everything was scarce with them, even words! No, my grandmother didn't

speaker: CG
words: talk with me. I can't remember any time that she ever really talked

speaker: CG
words: with me. And I did her housework for her for several months one time,

speaker: CG
words: but she would speak to me, if necessary, but she didn't talk with me.

speaker: CG
words: But my grandfather— how he came to be my grandfather—he was the beau

speaker: CG
words: of his community, away back when. And Grandmother was the belle of her

speaker: CG
words: community. My grandfather heard about that belle Sally Lillie— he'd

speaker: CG
words: go over and break her heart and go back home! Grandmother heard of that

speaker: CG
words: beau, Elias Connor, "Huh, he didn't need to come seeing her!" So,

speaker: CG
words: Elias left his community and rode his horse over to Sally's community

speaker: CG
words: Huh, six weeks later they were married. (Chuckles) Their first morning

speaker: CG
words: in their new home, Grandfather went out to take care of his saddle hor

speaker: CG
words: se, and Grandmother was going to show him how lively she was, how she

speaker: CG
words: could get around and do things. She'd have breakfast ready when he got

speaker: CG
words: that horse taken care of!! So, she fried a skillet of eggs on the fire

speaker: CG
words: place, which was the only way of having fire they had. She fried a skil

speaker: CG
words: let of eggs and pulled it back onto the hearth of the fireplace, and

speaker: CG
words: turned around and stepped in it! (Laughter) Her children never got ov

speaker: CG
words: er teasing her about that. But, anyway, they lived together for more

speaker: CG
words: than fifty years. And, as usual, she got up very early in the morning,

speaker: CG
words: got the breakfast, washed it up; got the dinner, washed it up; got the

speaker: CG
words: supper, washed it up; went to bed and died instantly. After, I think it

speaker: CG
words: was about fifty-four years they were together. And Grandfather expres

speaker: CG
words: sed himself this way, "I have a good home, I have one of my children

speaker: CG
words: still with me, but I am utterly bankrupt." That was his way of expres

speaker: CG
words: sing his thought for the death of his wife.

speaker: SS
words: Sounds like they must have had a very good marriage.

speaker: CG
words: They had a good marriage. And, as far as the Womens Christian Temper

speaker: CG
words: ance Union is concerned; he drank quite a bit when he was young. And

speaker: CG
words: when some of his children were teenagers, there was an evangelist came

speaker: CG
words: to their community in Minnesota and Grandfather and Grandmother and

speaker: CG
words: some of the children were thoroughly converted from the life they'd had

speaker: CG
words: to a Christian life, a Grandfather never drank another drop in his life!

speaker: CG
words: And none of his children drank. And Grandmother was all for everything

speaker: CG
words: that could be done to fight the liquor traffic. She made her mark in

speaker: CG
words: her community. And, by nature, she was not a mother; she was never in

speaker: CG
words: tended for a mother. But she was the most marvelous gardener that the

speaker: CG
words: world has ever known. When they moved to South Dakota it is said that

speaker: CG
words: the government report of the annual rainfall for that portion of South

speaker: CG
words: Dakota was fourteen inches. Well, that's below anything that is sup

speaker: CG
words: posed to be able to raise a crop. But, have a garden, this woman would!

speaker: CG
words: The very first thing she did was to begin to pick up stones and she did

speaker: CG
words: n't have to look anywhere for them; they were all over everywhere. She

speaker: CG
words: picked up stones and built a stone wall, oh, probably nearly three feet

speaker: CG
words: high around a plot right by the house for her garden.

speaker: CG
words: Well, when I knew it, I wasn't even in South Dakota, Territory, when we went

speaker: CG
words: there. But she built the stone wall around this garden plot, and at

speaker: CG
words: the time that I knew it, in the fall she would have several inches deep

speaker: CG
words: of litter from the cattle barn spread all over her garden and left there

speaker: CG
words: until spring. The garden was plowed as deep as possible, getting this

speaker: CG
words: litter mixed into the soil. Then after it was plowed with the two-horse

speaker: CG
words: plow as deep as possible, she had it raked and raked and raked with the

speaker: CG
words: horse rake. They went over and over and over that with the horse rake.

speaker: CG
words: Then, when she would finally let the horse rake go, she took the hand

speaker: CG
words: rake and she hand raked every inch of it. And only then, could seeds

speaker: CG
words: be put in the ground. But when that little old lady put a seed in the

speaker: CG
words: ground and said, "Now you grow!" it grew and it produced! She had, in

speaker: CG
words: that dry country— they had no water except what was hauled in a bar

speaker: CG
words: so you can be sure she didn't water her garden, and she didn't raise

speaker: CG
words: plants in the house, because there was no place in the house to put a

speaker: CG
words: plant, for one thing. But she planted the seeds right in the ground

speaker: CG
words: and she had tomatoes, watermelons and muskmelons— they were not canta

speaker: CG
words: loupe at that time, they were muskmelon—and finally they degraded into

speaker: CG
words: cantaloupe. And asparagus. And she would plant a few potatoes, just

speaker: CG
words: for early new potatoes. And she had corn aplenty, and lettuce and rad

speaker: CG
words: ish. She had one radish that was known as a winter radish, and it got

speaker: CG
words: to be about six inches long and, well, about half an inch in diameter

speaker: CG
words: and it was white and she took great pride in it at harvesttime having

speaker: CG
words: those radishes on the table for the harvesters. And they were good and

speaker: CG
words: sweet and tender at that time of year. And I had the seed of them at

speaker: CG
words: one time, but I finally lost it. Now, talking about that—

speaker: SS
words: May I just ask you one thing here? I take it gardening was her pride

speaker: SS
words: and joy, was it especially

speaker: CG
words: That was her pride and,,joy! Was gardening. And there wasn't anything

speaker: CG
words: that anybody could tell or knew about gardening that she didn't already

speaker: CG
words: know! And she had currant bushes that were just loaded, and she sold

speaker: CG
words: the currants, and she sold her garden stuff, too, so that she had a lit

speaker: CG
words: tle supply of cash of her own all the time. Uncle Will would hitch Old

speaker: CG
words: Kate to the buggy. Kate couldn't run away if she'd wanted to, and Grand

speaker: CG
words: mother would hold the lines— I wouldn't say she drove— but she held

speaker: CG
words: the lines and Old Kate took her to town and around the little streets

speaker: CG
words: and she sold her gardeware that way. And she had just about everything

speaker: CG
words: that is raised in a garden.

speaker: SS
words: Was this a lifelong interest of hers, or one that she developed in her

speaker: SS
words: later years?

speaker: CG
words: I think she had been born with a hoe and rake in her hands! And she

speaker: CG
words: had gardened before she went to South Dakota. She had gardened in Min

speaker: CG
words: nesota. And she had one hoe that she used for more than fifty years!

speaker: CG
words: And it was worn down until the blade was only half the usual width. But

speaker: CG
words: the things she could do with that hoe!! She would get up at four o'clock

speaker: CG
words: in the morning and go right out to her garden and start hoeing, and

speaker: CG
words: when she would get it hoed from this corner to that, she would come

speaker: CG
words: right back and start again at this corner. She hoed continually! She

speaker: CG
words: said there was water in the ground; she was going to have it up where

speaker: CG
words: her plants could get it and she did! She proved her theory. And, her

speaker: CG
words: gardening ability showed up in many of her descendants. Many of them

speaker: CG
words: were great gardeners. I'm not a great gardener and never was, but I

speaker: CG
words: do kno w an experiment that possibly if our population continues to in

speaker: CG
words: crease so rapidly, and if the adverse elements continue taking land

speaker: CG
words: away from the common people into government hands, this thing that I

speaker: CG
words: know maybe a real lifesaver.

speaker: CG
words: It is this: Up near Everett, Washington,

speaker: CG
words: a little town there, a man was given, what the one who gave it to him

speaker: CG
words: said was a separate kind of potato— just Irish potato—that you plant

speaker: CG
words: deep in the ground and keep covering it til you get to ground level

speaker: CG
words: with the cover, and at each joint it will put out a crop of potatoes.

speaker: CG
words: Well, this man planted the potato, oh, perhaps a foot and a half or

speaker: CG
words: even deeper in a hole in the ground and at Everett, in that country up

speaker: CG
words: there, the soil is bigwoods loam, which never hardens like the clay.

speaker: CG
words: And, he harvested almost one bushel of potatoes from that one little

speaker: CG
words: hole in the ground. Well, I had that seed, and I had it for years un

speaker: CG
words: til I had ten years of illness—did quite a lot to me— and during that

speaker: CG
words: time I lost the seed of it. But, when I was able again to do some gar

speaker: CG
words: dening I planted a common potato and it performed in the same way. A

speaker: CG
words: year ago I had a student gardening my ground here in Moscow and I plan

speaker: CG
words: ted just a common potato in a hole that he dug and I was going to keep

speaker: CG
words: track exactly of the produce from it, but low and behold, he,an Ag major,

speaker: CG
words: senior, without my consent or knowledge, just to be good to me, at po

speaker: CG
words: tato digging time, went out and dug that up and never kept any record

speaker: CG
words: at all of anything about it, an Ag student, a senior!! So I don't know

speaker: CG
words: just how performed, excepting that when I happened to go out and found

speaker: CG
words: what he was doing, he was almost through with it, he had on the ground

speaker: CG
words: about four good sized potatoes and a large number of very small po

speaker: CG
words: tatoes of various small sizes; they were really small, and it was so

speaker: CG
words: many— I was 50 furious at him doing this, although he had meant

speaker: CG
words: it for kindness, that I wouldn't ask him anything about it. I wouldn't

speaker: CG
words: speak to him about it. But he had so many of these small ones, that

speaker: CG
words: it looked to me as though they could not have come from one joint, they

speaker: CG
words: must have been from more than one joint, because there certainly were

speaker: CG
words: twenty or more of them.

speaker: SS
words: —WCTU— When did you yourself become interested in the WCTU, and become

speaker: SS
words: an active member?

speaker: CG
words: I became a member of the WCTU after I came to Moscow. And, it is a fact

speaker: CG
words: that I didn't join when I was first asked to join because the dues were

speaker: CG
words: a dollar and I didn't have quite dollars enough to feed myself and my

speaker: CG
words: daughter as we should have been fed. So I waited two or three years

speaker: CG
words: until I had a dollar I could spare for the dues. And that's more than

speaker: CG
words: forty years ago, I don't remember what year it was, but I've been here

speaker: CG
words: forty-eight years, and I think it's about forty two or three years ago

speaker: CG
words: perhaps, that I joined. And I have now given up most of the activities

speaker: CG
words: of it, excepting that I have charge of the devotions at every meeting.

speaker: CG
words: Not because I want to give up, but ninety-six years makes you give up

speaker: CG
words: quite a number of things, whether you will or no.

speaker: SS
words: And your mother was active in WCTU before you?

speaker: CG
words: My mother was jail visitor in Redfield, South Dakota. And for years

speaker: CG
words: I was jail visitor here in Moscow. And I confined my jail visiting to

speaker: CG
words: the juveniles. There was one juvenile, I wouldn't mention his name if

speaker: CG
words: I did remember,names and faces have no meaning for me any more. They

speaker: CG
words: don't register. I remember the person, all the incidents, but the name

speaker: CG
words: and the face don't register.

speaker: SS
words: In some ways that's the least important part anyway.

speaker: CG
words: But there was this one boy who was a perfectly good boy, but a bunch—

speaker: CG
words: a group, and this was a blood-sworn group of boys— can you feature it?-

speaker: CG
words: just young boys in grade school being blood-sworn? Every one of them

speaker: CG
words: had had his wrist slit for blood token of his loyalty to the group. Well,

speaker: CG
words: this boy was not that kind of a boy at all, either by raising or by

speaker: CG
words: nature, but they heeded, a stool pigeon, and they got him in some way

speaker: CG
words: or other and he didn't know how to get out. And when it came to a mat

speaker: CG
words: ter of the officers, low and behold, here's this stool pigeon to put up.

speaker: CG
words: And I visited him in jail. And the sheriff was very considerate. He

speaker: CG
words: took me and the boy into his private office and let us talk there. Well,

speaker: CG
words: the boy was— in spite of the fact that he nearly bit his tongue out

speaker: CG
words: trying to avoid it— he was crying bitterly. His ideal was that every

speaker: CG
words: thing for him was over; there was no future; there was nothing. Well,

speaker: CG
words: I talked to him and showed him that there was a future. That he could

speaker: CG
words: recover from this and finally got him comforted and quieted. And I

speaker: CG
words: kept track of him the while that he was in the jail. During this time

speaker: CG
words: I was cooking on the campus, but, anyway, found time to visit this

speaker: CG
words: boy in the evening. And at that time, the sheriff would let a jail visi

speaker: CG
words: tor in at any time that she could be there, so there was no question on

speaker: CG
words: that. When I'd get home in the evening, I'd go visit this boy every

speaker: CG
words: now and then. He was in the jail a while. And they sent him to the re

speaker: CG
words: form school.

speaker: SS
words: Did he actually commit a crime?

speaker: CG
words: He was with the boys who did, and how much of it he did, I do not know.

speaker: CG
words: But he was with the boys.

speaker: SS

speaker: SS
words: Well, he was just in this position that he was jailed, and they had to

speaker: SS
words: keep him, and I am sure that the sheriff, himself, saw the proposition

speaker: SS
words: as it was; that he was merely the stool pigeon who had been taken in

words: for the purpose of being taken, and he was with the boys when they com

speaker: SS
words: mitted whatever it was, I do not remember. But, whether he actually

words: was a committant, I do not know. I didn't try to find out. But anyway,

speaker: SS
words: he came back to Moscow discharged from the home, he had a good home

words: here, he had a good Christian Mother, had been well raised. As soon

speaker: SS
words: as he came he came to see me and I kept track of him. Well, as years

words: went on, he grew up and married a good girl; had a nice family, and he

words: brought me home from a church nursery, that I had charge of— he brought

words: me home from there one day with his son with him; I don't remember, per

speaker: SS
words: haps he had two children with him, I know he had a son with as he brought

speaker: SS
words: me home. And his son was a lively rascal and he wanted to know if I

speaker: SS
words: knew his father, and I said, "Yes,"I knew his father. Well, I could

speaker: SS
words: see instantly that the father's record was not known to the children

speaker: SS
words: and that he was afraid that it would leak out now. (Chuckles) I took a

speaker: SS
words: lot of care that it didn't leak out. It was his business, not mine,

speaker: SS
words: whether this record was known to the children. He was just on nettles

speaker: SS
words: for a minute for fear of an answer that I would make to the boy, but I

speaker: SS
words: made a round about answer that quieted the boy, and said nothing.

speaker: SS

speaker: CG
words: oil or gas or something in Troy. And he was sent to the Home. I visit

speaker: CG
words: ed him while he was in the jail here. And I wrote to him all the time.

speaker: CG
words: And one time I said, "Now is there anything you want, that I could send

speaker: CG
words: you?" He replied, "Yes, I'm building a chest and I want you to send me

words: a lock that nobody can open!" (Laughter) Evidently he didn't remem

speaker: CG
words: ber that he'd opened a lock! Because he had opened a lock to get in.

speaker: SS
words: Was this position of "gail walker"— this was a responsibility that the

speaker: SS
words: WCTU had a person take on?

speaker: CG
words: It's a separate department. We have a lot of departments of work. And

speaker: CG
words: this was one department, and the "jail visitor" carried it on in her

speaker: CG
words: own way. On one of my organizing trips in South Idaho, I went to a Sun

speaker: CG
words: day morning service at the jail with the jail visitor there. And every

speaker: CG
words: Sunday morning at this hour, she went to the jail and the sheriff had

speaker: CG
words: the men all congregated where she could stand in the hall with every-

speaker: CG
words: thing between the men— the men were not given any liberty at all, they

speaker: CG
words: were confined-— but they were all in one place and she could stand in

speaker: CG
words: the hall outside, and she sang with them and prayed with them, and talk

speaker: CG
words: ed with them. And that was her regular Sunday morning service as jail

speaker: CG
words: visitor. And each one carries on the department in her own way. My

speaker: CG
words: way was to visit them personally. I visited one boy in the jail here—

speaker: CG
words: there was something wrong with his mentality—to destroy and steal and

speaker: CG
words: kill. It was the only thing he knew anything about. But, anyway, I

speaker: CG
words: went to visit him and the sheriff said that he really was dangerous,

speaker: CG
words: but if I wanted to I might talk with him. I said, "Yes, I would like

speaker: CG
words: to talk with him." So the sheriff took me upstairs to the boy's cell

speaker: CG
words: and put me in the cell and locked the door. And, I had just gotten the

speaker: CG
words: boy from his braggadocio mood to where he could make some response to

speaker: CG
words: what I was saying to him, when the jailer got worried about it, because

speaker: CG
words: the time had gone along, and he came up to see if I was still alive or

speaker: CG
words: not. He said when they took the boy he was carrying a six shooter.

speaker: CG
words: And he was just a kid. But, anyway, I didn't go back to see this boy

speaker: CG
words: because, apparently there was nothing in the brief time that he would

speaker: CG
words: be here that anybody could do, that would have been lasting with him.

speaker: CG
words: He was just mentally down. But, we do have— One woman said to me the

speaker: CG
words: other day, she said, "Well, I don't care so much about your WCTU. All

speaker: CG
words: you ever do is just to fight booze." "Oh," I said, ,"I don't believe

speaker: CG
words: it is." I said, "It happens that the woman who created the WCTU was

speaker: CG
words: responsible for the eight hour law, and for the child labor law." I

speaker: CG
words: said, "We do quite a lot of things besides fight booze, although we do

speaker: CG
words: fight it tooth and toenail all of the time." We do have all these dep

speaker: CG
words: artments, and at present, whether they have found the person or not, I

speaker: CG
words: don't know, but we are looking— if they haven't already found it— we

speaker: CG
words: are looking for a family, a whole family, that absolutely cannot buy

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: we may send them a dinner. We do that each year. Last year we sent

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: you want to give somebody something to find the person who ought to

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: simply could not buy a dinner. But, we were given two names; one of

speaker: CG
words: them was a family where the mother worked, the father was invalid and

speaker: CG
words: while they got along, it was just to the end of everything every pay

speaker: CG
words: check. And the other one was a woman whose husband was incarcerated

speaker: CG
words: eiVWer in Idaho or somewhere, I presume in Boise, and they were not only

words: dinners that they give— you probably know about that—they were to

words: we do?" I said, "We were given the name, let's give her the dinner any

words: way." And, I said, "This other one that you mention, since they are

speaker: CG
words: sort of provided for, but just on a shoestring basis,"I said, "let's

speaker: CG

words: but the saloon was getting it. And we had been given that name. And,

words: turkey. "Why," she said, "I don't want a dinner given me." I said,

speaker: CG

words: signation was. She said, "Yes, but I thought it merely meant some littie

words: ''Why don't you put your name in? I put mine, why don't you put yours.'

words: to make the day for the children." "But," she said, "I will not take a

words: dinner. I wouldn't think of it!" Well, that place was providing din

speaker: CG
words: ners, that's what they were for, was dinners, but she had thought dif

speaker: CG
words: No. No, we weren't giving dinners for Christmas. We were just giving

words: anyway. But, anyway, there was another place that had been given me.

speaker: CG

words: half a block from the place, we knew it by the odor. Oh! It was ter

speaker: CG
words: rible! Well, I hadn't visited them before, I was just simply taking

speaker: CG
words: the treats. So, I did the best I could about the odor and went to the

speaker: CG
words: door. Well, it was very plain to me what the place was for. I didn't

speaker: CG
words: know whether the man at the back door had just come in and was inter

words: all people, but still to express it, it was a place kept for other than

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: the family; that the son had a fulltime job; he was a moron— that he

words: then the daughter was making this extra money. And, yet, we were asked

speaker: CG

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: But she didn't get up from her cot, where she was lying.

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: the one they left. And, at least, where they were can be cleaned up."

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: I've seen way back in Redfield when I was a kid— Redfield, South Dakota-

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: Sometimes it'd be a businessman in his business suit; sometimes it'd be

speaker: CG
words: some bedraggled tramp in his Levis, although they weren't Levis at that

speaker: CG
words: time, they were overalls. But, Mother fed everyone who came. Someone

speaker: CG
words: said to me one time, "Well, your mother has no business to do that, she'll

speaker: CG
words: be found dead there by one of those fellows some of these days." Well,

speaker: CG
words: I ran home as hard as I could go and told Mother. And Mother said, "Well,

speaker: CG
words: there is no man as dangerous as a hungry man." And she went right on

speaker: CG
words: feeding them! But, really, it was pitiful. It was hardttimes after that

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: when the landlady was gone, and I tended the door for her when she was

speaker: CG
words: gone, a man came to the door. He was just a common businessman in his

speaker: CG
words: business suit. It was very apparent that he was not a tramp in any way

speaker: CG
words: whatever, and he asked me if he could have something to eat. Well, my

speaker: CG
words: self and a little girl in an apartment just for a little short time—

speaker: CG
words: but I found something for him anyway. And, he said to me, "Do you have

speaker: CG
words: any coffee?" I said, "No, I never drink it. I don't have any in the

speaker: CG
words: house." Oh, was so disappointed. (Chuckles) And at one time, later "'

speaker: CG
words: than that, I was at Heath, Montana in the country. He had evidently

speaker: CG
words: lost his means and something about a picture machine— well, at that

speaker: CG
words: time, a picture machine was a very new thing; the movies, it was very

speaker: CG
words: new. And he would mumble to himself about a picture machine. Well, it

speaker: CG
words: was plain that his mind was turned. Well, here I was, alone with a lit

speaker: CG
words: tle child in the country, I didn't know whether he was going to go or

speaker: CG
words: not! But finally, he did go on. But a person meets all kinds.

speaker: SS

speaker: SS
words: had some means to lose what they had? Was it just hard times in the

speaker: SS
words: country? What happened?

speaker: CG
words: The banks were all closed, with all the person had in the bank. He had

speaker: CG
words: just what he had in his pocket. Just one bank after another went out.

speaker: CG
words: And it was real hard times. At your age you've never seen real hard

speaker: CG
words: times! But, there were people who had been living in comfort that when

speaker: CG
words: they ate what was in the house;they were out! I remember one time, we

speaker: CG
words: were living on a farm at Geyser, Montana, near Geyser, and there were

speaker: CG
words: two men came along. We were seven miles from town. I don't know, maybe

speaker: CG
words: they came out with my husband when he drove out from town. Anyway, they

speaker: CG
words: were there to supper. My husband gave them a blanket and they slept

speaker: CG
words: in the manger. One of them was a tramp. I don't see tramps any more.

speaker: CG
words: We used to have them, they were known as tramps and they were tramps.

speaker: CG
words: They had no other destination than some place that isn't here! And

speaker: CG
words: they had that all^the time. And they lived on what they could get at

speaker: CG
words: back doors. But, anyway, these two men were there at our ranch and

speaker: CG
words: they came in and supper with us. And then, they had this blanket

speaker: CG
words: and slept in the manger and they came in to breakfast and then they went

speaker: CG
words: on. One of them was thoroughly a tramp, and the other was absolutely

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: this businessman was out like this, 'it is possible that he was running

speaker: CG
words: from the law. He may have been in on some crooked deal, I don't know,

speaker: CG
words: that was as near as I could get p,t- any guess that seemed reasonable.

speaker: CG
words: But, anyway, the two men were so different, but when they had their break

speaker: CG
words: fast they just wandered on. But why they would be here seven miles from

speaker: CG
words: town— so I thought probably the man might have been running from the

speaker: CG
words: law.

words: door, was it common for someone to come to the door? Knock on the door

words: wondered about it ever since. I happened to be looking out, it was

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: hand, was coming up the drive, and I wondered who or what. So I stood

speaker: CG
words: looking out the window, and he went to the steps that go up the bank,

words: of the garage, and he looked up there quite a little bit and then

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: said,— he wanted to know if there was a Mission in this town. I said

speaker: CG
words: "well, but what kind of a mission?" "The kind that have dinners, that

speaker: CG
words: kind, I haven't eaten much for the last two days." And I said, "No,

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: step and I'll bring you something." Well, it happened that I had just

speaker: CG
words: a very little bread in the house. And one person alone, one old woman

words: doesn't have a lot of food around like families do, but, anyway, I took

speaker: CG

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: Well, you know, some people drink it and some don't. And he said, "If

speaker: CG
words: it wouldn't be too much trouble." I said, "It's no trouble at all."

speaker: CG

words: neck he had a strap with a billfold— not a billfold but a briefcase—

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: You see, I had come in '27 and it was '40 before I had got financially

speaker: CG

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: cents an hour. I have to this day some cupboard shelves and doors that

speaker: CG
words: out— and when he got through, he was a very rapid worker, when he got

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: cents!" And he had put up two sets of shelves with doors. Well, I gave

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: thought, "Yes, and you don't have any home either." So, I said, "Well,

speaker: CG

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: I wasn't so awfully flush myself, I was feeding college men and while I

speaker: CG
words: had a profit every month, it wasn't big by any means. But you know, that

speaker: CG

speaker: CG

words: this country— Paul Rice; Dr. Paul Rice. He was getting his masters at

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: I had two frosh here a while, and I wasn't making any profit on those

speaker: CG
words: two frosh! They were farm boys, both of them, and it took about as much

speaker: CG
words: to feed the two as it did the others, all the others. So one day, one

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: about it, he said, "Mrs. Grove, we found a place where we can get lodg

speaker: CG
words: Wayne, if I knew a place like that, I'd go and take it and shut this up!"

speaker: CG

speaker: CG

speaker: SS

speaker: CG
words: No. No, they just ate, just the same as at a restaurant, that was all.

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: "how about an egg?" I said, "Just fine." He said, "Do you think I

speaker: CG

speaker: CG

speaker: CG
words: wise .

speaker: CG

words: ial. There was Floyd, who was teaching in the high school, and Paul, who

speaker: CG
